perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any and all information (including , without limitation, all business, financial, commerci al and technical information and data) , in whatever form transmitted, whether tangible or intangible, that is disclosed, furnished or otherwise provided or made available by the d isclosing p arty to the receiving p arty prior to or after the date of this Agreement . “Confidential Information” includes, without limitation, design documents, trade secrets, files, marketing and business plans, documentation, reports, projections, interpretations, forecasts, records (including information regarding the disclosing party’s businesses, customers, strategies, operations, finances, technology, data, processes, methodologies, know -how, existing or future products, services, applications and methods of operation (including how such methods are developed, conducted or operated)) and all other information, whether in written, oral, encoded, electronic or other tangible or intangible form, and whether or not labeled, marked or otherwise identified as “Confidential” upon disclosure thereof, relating to the disclosing party’s products and businesses , and any other information that would, if disclosed to any actual or potential competitors of the disclosing party , give or increase those competitors’ advantage over the d isclosing party or directly or indirectly harm the disclosin g party’s business.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means information directly related to ongoing business discussions. This includes documents explicitly marked as 'Confidential'.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' was broad, covering various forms of data. The modified definition significantly narrows this scope, limiting it to information 'directly related to ongoing business discussions' and 'documents explicitly marked as Confidential.' This omission of the comprehensive definition creates an in-text contradiction because later sections assume the broader original definition, particularly Section 2 which discusses the receiving party's obligations regarding 'all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.justification: The modified definition of 'Confidential Information' significantly narrows its scope compared to the original text, only including information 'directly related to ongoing business discussions' and 'documents explicitly marked as Confidential'. This creates a strong in-text contradiction with the contradicted text, which discusses the receiving party's obligations regarding 'all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party'. The narrower definition in the changed text directly conflicts with the broader assumption of what constitutes 'Confidential Information' elsewhere in the agreement, making it unclear which information is actually protected and therefore causing a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any trade secrets will be entitled to all of the protections and benefits of applicable trade secret laws and any other applicable laws. For avoidance of doubt, the parties acknowledge and agree that, in the event a court of competent jurisdiction determines that information the disclosing p arty deems to be a trade secret is not a trade secret under applicable law, such information will nevertheless be deemed to be Confidential Information for purposes hereof, provided such information falls within the description set forth in Section 1(a).
perturbation.changed_text: Any trade secrets will be entitled to all of the protections and benefits of applicable trade secret laws and any other applicable laws.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the part of the clause that ensures information deemed confidential but not technically a trade secret by a court is still protected under this agreement, an in-text contradiction arises. The modified text now omits the provision for continued protection, leading to potential legal uncertainty if a 'trade secret' is challenged. The contradiction occurs because Section 2 still refers to the broader definition of 'Confidential Information', which used to include items that could have been deemed trade secrets under section 1(a) and the second half of the original clause. Now the contract becomes unclear on whether such challenged information should be protected or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the provision that information deemed confidential but not technically a trade secret by a court is still protected. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which obligates the receiving party to protect 'Confidential Information', including information that falls under the description in Section 1(a) which was present in the original text. By removing the original text's clarification, the contract now becomes unclear on whether challenged information should be protected or not, directly conflicting with the receiving party's obligations outlined in the Contradicted Text, thus fulfilling the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, “Confidential Information” shall not mean information that, as demonstrate d by the receiving party , is (i) in the public domain prior to the date of this Agreement through no wrongful act of the receiving party or its agents or employees, (ii) independently developed by the receiving party without any use of, or reference to, Confidential Information, (iii) rightfully furnished to the receiving party by any third party without violation or breach of any confidentiality restriction, or (iv) authorized in writing for release by the disclosing party .
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, “Confidential Information” shall not mean information that, as demonstrate d by the receiving party , is (i) in the public domain prior to the date of this Agreement through no wrongful act of the receiving party or its agents or employees, or (ii) authorized in writing for release by the disclosing party .
perturbation.explanation: By removing clauses (ii) and (iii), which stated that 'independently developed' information and information 'rightfully furnished by a third party' were not considered Confidential Information, the scope of Confidential Information is expanded. This creates an in-text contradiction with the general understanding of confidentiality agreements, as parties might assume they can use independently developed information. This contradicts the expectations set up in Section 2(a)(iii) which restricts using any Confidential Information without prior written consent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes clauses that previously excluded 'independently developed' information and information 'rightfully furnished by a third party' from being considered Confidential Information. This expansion of what constitutes Confidential Information directly contradicts Section 2(a)(iii) which states the receiving party shall 'refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose' without prior written consent. Now, independently developed information or information from third parties, which were previously not considered confidential, are now implicitly considered confidential and cannot be used without consent, creating a direct conflict and thus a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq.,w ithout the written consent of the disclos ing party, the receiving party will not disclose the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any person other than the receiving party’s officers, directors, owners, employees, agents and representatives (i) with a valid need to know such Confidenti al Information and (ii) that have an enforceable agreement with the receiving party containing confidentiality obligations substantially similar to those terms and conditions applicable to the receiving party under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq.,w ithout the written consent of the disclos ing party, the receiving party will not disclose the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any person other than the receiving party’s officers, directors, owners, employees, agents and representatives (i) with a valid need to know such Confidenti al Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that individuals receiving confidential information have an enforceable confidentiality agreement, the modified text implies that internal personnel with a 'need to know' are free to disclose the information without consequence, as long as they have a 'need to know'. This creates an in-text contradiction because the overall agreement is centered around maintaining confidentiality, and this clause weakens that core principle. This directly contradicts Section 2(a)(i) which states that the receiving party shall 'hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires that individuals receiving confidential information have an enforceable confidentiality agreement. The changed text removes this requirement, implying that internal personnel with a 'need to know' are free to disclose the information without consequence, as long as they have a 'need to know'. This contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the receiving party shall 'hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence'. The removal of the confidentiality agreement requirement directly conflicts with the obligation to hold information in strict confidence, creating a strong in-text contradiction. This aligns with the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the disclosing party’s request , all documents, materials and other items containing the disclosing party’s Confidential Information (including without limitation, all originals, copies, and reproductions of the Confidential Information) shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party without retaining a copy thereof, an d the receiving party shall provide a certification , signed by an officer, as to the completeness of the return of such material s. Upon such request, the receiving party also shall destroy all documents, materials and other items embodying or summarizing the disclosing party’s Confidential Information in wha tever format, and shall provide a similar certification as to the completeness of the destruction of such materials. Compliance with this Subsection 2(e) shall not relieve the receiving party of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the disclosing party’s request , all documents, materials and other items containing the disclosing party’s Confidential Information (including without limitation, all originals, copies, and reproductions of the Confidential Information) shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party without retaining a copy thereof. Compliance with this Subsection 2(e) shall not relieve the receiving party of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirements for a signed certification of completeness for returned and destroyed materials, the burden of proof is shifted entirely to the disclosing party. This creates an in-text contradiction regarding enforcement. While the receiving party is still obligated to return/destroy materials, the disclosing party now has no guaranteed mechanism to verify compliance beyond taking the receiving party's word, undermining the accountability intended in the agreement. This is in contrast to section 2(a)(ii) that specifies the receiving party should take precautions to avoid the loss or misuse of the confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.justification: The original text included a requirement for a signed certification of completeness for returned and destroyed materials. This requirement was removed in the changed text. According to the explanation, this shifts the burden of proof to the disclosing party, undermining the accountability intended in the agreement and creating a contradiction. Specifically, it contradicts Section 2(a)(ii) of the contradicted text, which requires the receiving party to take reasonable security precautions to avoid disclosure, loss, or misuse of the confidential information. The removal of the certification process weakens the ability to ensure these precautions are effectively implemented, creating an in-text contradiction as the means of verification are removed.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledge s that the other party’s Confidential Information is highly valuable, confidential and proprietary to the other party and has been developed through the investment of significant time, effort and expense, and that maintaining the confidentiality of this Confidential Information is essential for the general successful operations of TTU and Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledge s that the other party’s Confidential Information is considered information needing to be safeguarded, and that maintaining confidentiality is essential for TTU and Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit recognition of the 'highly valuable, confidential and proprietary' nature of the information and the 'significant time, effort and expense' invested in its development, the modified text diminishes the implied level of protection. The use of 'considered information needing to be safeguarded' is weaker language. This creates an in-text contradiction with the strict obligations outlined in Section 2 regarding the handling of Confidential Information, as the acknowledgment now suggests a lower priority for safeguarding the information. It undermines the sense of urgency around the protection defined in section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the acknowledgment of the importance and value of the Confidential Information by replacing stronger language ('highly valuable, confidential and proprietary', 'significant time, effort and expense') with weaker language ('considered information needing to be safeguarded'). This creates an in-text contradiction with the strict obligations outlined in the Contradicted Text, which requires the receiving party to hold the information in 'trust and strict confidence' and take 'reasonable security precautions'. The lessened emphasis on the value of the information in the Changed Text undermines the justification for the stringent protective measures detailed in the Contradicted Text, making it a clear and impactful contradiction.
